summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an upcoming protest at JP Morgan headquarters organized by her OWS Alt Banking group as part of a broader 'Week of Action' during New York's mayoral transition. She reflects on how Occupy's role has evolved from provoking outrage to inspiring hope for positive change. The post describes the coalition effort and previews protest signs, including one referencing a recent viral Twitter confrontation with JPMorgan. She notes the challenge of communicating complex financial critique (like the London Whale scandal) to a general audience.
key_points: The protest is part of a coalition effort timed to NYC's Bloomberg-to-de Blasio transition, framing it as a moment for civic change
Occupy's messaging has shifted from provoking outrage to inspiring hope that 'things could be a whole lot better'
The 'be in the know' theme emphasizes financial education over pure confrontation
Complex financial scandals like the London Whale require too much context to communicate effectively on protest signs
money_quotes: There's so much widespread distrust and hatred of the big banks at this point that I feel like Occupy's role has gone from provoking people to be outraged to provoking people to be hopeful.
Hopeful about the fact that things could be a whole lot better than this, if we work together.
I also made a sign that referenced the London Whale and the risk model, but someone said we might need to give people a copy of our recent book, Occupy Finance, just to understand that sign.
key_insight: As public anger at big banks became mainstream, Occupy's role evolved from sparking outrage to channeling that energy into hope for collective change.
